# Extracao: transcription_mp4_01-_WEBINAR

- **Fonte:** transcription_mp4_01-_WEBINAR.txt
- **Tipo:** TXT
- **Tamanho:** 0.03 MB

---

So I'm here today to talk about origin stories. I'm not here to talk about how to tell a story. I've got plenty of courses on that. You can learn that in a lot of places. I'm here today to talk about finding and assembling the correct origin story, whether that origin story is for you as a human being, an entrepreneur, a CEO, just a creator of some kind, or you're looking for the origin story of your business or your startup. If you're trying to find that story that you tell people that explains who you are or what your company is, essentially the DNA, the birth of you and the formation of you or the birth of your company and the development of your company, that is what your origin story is. And it's important. It's a story you have to tell often, and it's a story you should be telling often. It's the story that explains who you are and how you got here and perhaps where you're going in the future. The tricky thing is when people come to me and they ask me to help them with their origin story, they don't use the plural of the word story. Instead, they think they have one origin story. And we're going to talk today about how that is not the case. I've got some bread here. I've got a loaf of bread. We're going to use this as the metaphor. The problem with origin stories is people often think of their lives as a loaf of bread, and that's perfectly fine. Sure, this is your life. This is all of the things that have encompassed your life or even all of the things that have encompassed the actual process of becoming who you are as a person or who you are as a company, a loaf of bread. But the problem is no one wants to eat a loaf of bread. This is a delicious bread. It's fantastic. But if you had to eat the whole loaf, you'd start to hate it. And that's what people do when they think of an origin story. They think it's just one and they think it's this. Let me tell you the story of my life as a writer, as a carpenter, as a plumber, as a filmmaker. Or they say, let me tell you the origin story of my company from the moment I thought of the company taking a shower one day all the way up to the point that we are now a multimillion dollar international brand. And that is a terrible thing to do to any audience. No one wants to eat a loaf of bread. The trick, the secret in origin stories, is you have many of them. We all do. And like bread, what we want to do is slice that loaf as thin as possible. We want to cut the loaf of bread. And what we want to end up with is the very thinnest slice possible. In other words, we want a multitude of origin stories. We want to have more than one. We want to have an enormous collection of stories that explain how we got from A to B, how we got from birth to where we are today, how we got from the first beginnings of our professional career or our career in business or our business itself all the way up to where we are now. The more slices we have, the better off we are. And it turns out that this is how people want to get a story. They don't want this. They want this. They want to know something about your company. They want to know a story that says something about you, but they don't want the whole story. Your mother wants the whole story. That's probably the only person who wants the whole story. So I'm going to talk about slicing that loaf of bread into pieces. I'm going to give you three strategies that you can use to begin slicing that loaf of bread that is your life or the life of your business into thinner pieces. Three strategies that you can begin using immediately to start finding those stories you need to tell. And then if you want to learn how to tell them, I've got plenty of resources for you to learn how to actually tell those stories. So let's talk about finding a multitude of origin stories and then what we'll do with them. Strategy number one is I want you to start thinking about origin stories in terms of chronology, in terms of time span. I want us to start thinking about our origins going back as far as possible. So I'm going to talk about myself. I today am a writer. I write novels. I write screenplays. I write poems. I am also a person who does stand-up comedy, and I tell stories on stages. I also teach. I teach storytelling. I teach fifth graders. I do a lot of things. But essentially what I do is I work in story and I work in sentences. I collect sentences and assemble them so that they eventually tell a story on the page, on the stage, in the classroom, in a workshop. That is sort of what I'm trying to speak to my audience. That's what I'm trying to explain. That is the loaf of bread that is me. But I'm not going to tell you the whole thing. I'm going to go find some stories. So if I think back as far as I possibly can, if I think back and say, where did I first start becoming invested in story? The earliest version of that is 10 years old. It's 1981. And E.T., The Extra-Terrestrial, is in the movie theaters. And my parents take me to see E.T. St. in the Theater in Winsocket, Rhode Island. I eat some popcorn and I watch Steven Spielberg's Masterpiece. And when we leave the theater, I am angry. I'm an angry 10-year-old boy. I'm angry because what I have seen is an almost perfect film. But there's one scene in that movie that I despise. One that I know is inauthentic and terrible and breaks the movie in my mind. It's a scene involving a science class where frogs are brought into the science class. And Elliot, our main character, is expected to kill a frog and then dissect the frog. As a kid, I know this is nonsense. There is no way in any school in America that kids are asked to murder their frog and then cut their frog open. And then there's a moment where Elliot decides he's not going to kill his frog. He's not going to kill any of the frogs. And he releases all the frogs. And then the frogs by some miracle, not through E.T.'s powers, but just through frog-like powers. They all seem to just hop in the direction of an open door in a classroom out into a field. As if any of this would happen. I watch that scene as a kid and I go, why did you do that? It was an unnecessary scene. It was a stupid scene and it made me crazy. So I went home and I wrote Spielberg a letter. The letter essentially said something to the effect of, I love your movies, Steven, I love them all, but it seems like in every movie you do one stupid thing. And I see that stupid thing. So I invite Steven Spielberg to allow me to watch his movies first. I tell Steven Spielberg at the age of 10 that he should send me the first cut of the movie so I can tell him all the stupid things he's doing so that when it actually gets to the movie theater, everything will be better. That's as far back as I can remember. The farthest back I can remember caring about a story. That is one slice of bread. That is one moment in my career as a storyteller and a sentence maker and a screenwriter and a novelist when I looked at a story and said, that's not right. Which is essentially what I do today. I sit down with people and businesses and I sit down with individuals, they tell me their stories and I tell them what's wrong. It's the same thing except I was 10 and I was watching ET. So that's one story in my slice of bread. Another one happens when I'm 17 years old and I'm sitting in an English class. I'm writing satire for the first time. It's the first time I really care about anything that I'm writing. I handed an assignment to Mr. Campo Piano thinking it's the most brilliant thing I've ever written in my life. In the grade I get back is a B minus. It says too obvious, not satire. And I lose my mind. I charge to the front of the classroom. I take on Mr. Campo. He takes me on. He's a short balled man. They tend to come in either the funny variety or the angry variety. He's always sort of the angry short balled man. So we're going at it in front of the class. Class gets really quiet. They're not accustomed to two people in the front of the class, particularly a teacher and a student yelling at each other. Mr. Campo says, stop, stop, stop. He says, read it to the class. We'll see what they think. If they agree that it's satire, I'll make you B minus and A minus. If they agree with me and it's too obvious, your B minus becomes a C minus. Brilliant. What a teacher. And so I stand in front of a classroom for the very first time in my life. I speak words to an audience, words that mean something to me. I read those words and as I read them, I hear a laugh. It's the laugh of the girl I have loved since sixth grade. A girl I can never have because she's with a boy who she shouldn't be with, but she laughs. It's the most beautiful sound I've ever heard. And then pretty soon the whole class is laughing. And at the end of that piece, Mr. Campo steps forward and says, raise your hand if you agree with what Matt has written as satire. And every hand goes up, including Mr. Campos. He tells me that on the page it reads pretty flat. But when I perform it, suddenly it becomes satire. In that moment, three things happen for me. The first is I make a girl laugh. It's basically why I work today. It's why I tell stories today. To keep my wife laughing, to convince her I'm worth having around. Number two, I make a teacher look foolish, which is a big deal for a 17 year old who wants to fight against authority all the time and for once in his life he actually wins. And number three, most important, I change my future. A kid who's about to get kicked out of his house. I'm about to live on my own, live on my own for the rest of my life, wondering where I'm gonna eat and where I'm gonna live. Suddenly I've written something, I've changed my future. Turned a B minus into an A minus. I still have the paper today. And so the next day I opened my very first business, writing term papers for my classmates. My first paid writing gig. That's the story at 17. I'm 27 now. I'm 27 and I'm in college. I'm trying to write novels and I'm failing. And I sort of quit. I come to some odd conclusion that I'm not equipped to write fiction. Maybe I'll write for magazines, I'll write memoir. Fiction isn't working for me. And I kind of stop writing to a great degree. I'm still writing every day, but not doing it like a professional. And so my friend, Shep, calls me one day and he says, "'Hey, I'm getting some guys together "'to play Dungeons and Dragons. "'Do you want to play?' "'Shep is clearly going through "'some kind of midlife crisis. "'It's fine.' "'But I tell Shep, no thank you. "'I say I played Dungeons and Dragons "'when I was a kid. "'I was actually a Dungeon Master. "'I was a storyteller playing Dungeons and Dragons, "'which is also a slice of bread. "'But I tell Shep that I was already "'a Dungeons and Dragons player. "'And now what I want to be "'is a guy who has a girlfriend. "'And I tell Shep, I don't think playing Dungeons and Dragons "'is gonna get me a girlfriend "'and I'm just not that interested in it.' "'And he says, that's fine.' "'Three days later he calls me and he says, "'Okay, so you don't want to play D&D, "'but what if you wrote the adventures for us? "'Write the modules that we'll play.' "'And I say, Shep, that sounds worse "'than playing Dungeons and Dragons. "'And Shep says to me, well, a while ago "'you told me that you were gonna write a novel. "'And as far as I can tell, "'you're not doing very much of anything right now. "'So if you're gonna waste your time doing nothing, "'why not waste your time writing for us?' "'You have a friend like that in your life, "'you're a very lucky person.' "'So I tell Shep, fine. "'I will write Dungeons and Dragons adventures for you.' "'So I go down to my basement, "'I pull out my old books because I still have them. "'I read them and I begin writing Dungeons and Dragons "'adventures for my friends. "'And they begin playing them in the basement at Shep's house. "'And sometimes I sit and I just watch "'and sometimes I act as the Dungeon Master. "'I do that for a year. "'And after a year of writing for an audience, "'I feel it again. "'I feel like I can write fiction. "'I feel like I wanna find the audience "'for the fiction I really wanna write. "'So I tell Shep one day, "'I'm not gonna write adventures for you anymore, "'I'm gonna write a novel. "'And Shep says, why don't you do both?' "'And I said, no. "'I said, I don't really like Dungeons and Dragons "'enough to write the adventures, "'but I'm gonna write a novel. "'And Shep says to me, "'when you have something you're proud of, "'I'll be your first reader.' "'And he is. "'When I write my first novel, "'Something Missing, within the next two years, "'Shep is the first reader of every chapter I write. "'That is a slice from my origin story.' "'I have 25 of those, "'25 moments in my life "'that have led me to the point I am at today, "'beginning all the way at age 10, "'all the way up until six months ago "'when I found a new slice. "'I've got all of those stories, "'and that is an enormously powerful thing to have.' "'I'm gonna talk about why in a little bit, "'but the key here is to think about your origin "'of your company or yourself, "'not through the lens of the last 10 years "'or the last five years or the last one year. "'When did you first begin walking the path "'that you're on today? "'When did that first glimmer of a future emerge? "'When I'm criticizing Steven Spielberg's E.T. "'when I'm 10 years old, I am not envisioning a career "'where I'm working for Microsoft and Amazon and the FBI, "'consulting with them on storytelling. "'I'm writing to a film director.' "'That letter, of course, never makes it to Steven Spielberg. "'There's no way a mother in 1981 "'can find the address for Amblin Entertainment "'and land that letter in Spielberg's lap. "'She probably throws it away. He never sees it. "'I don't see the future then, "'but in retrospect, I can see the beginnings of a future. "'That's what I want you to do. "'So your first task is chronological. "'Expand the time horizon. "'Think about how far back you can go. "'Those early stories tend to be some of the best. "'They tend to be some of the most inspiring. "'They tend to be some of the most unique "'and entertaining stories you can tell. "'So expand the time horizon. "'Think about going as far back as possible "'to the first step you took on this journey "'and then track every subsequent step along the way. "'Gather those stories. "'Pile up your slices of bread "'so that you have a multitude of stories to play with.'" Strategy number two, context switch. One of the tricky things about origin stories is they often don't necessarily involve the field that we are currently in or the business that we are currently in. And when we can context switch, when we can find other universes where we began our journey, that can be really entertaining. So I ask myself, I've been on this journey of becoming a sentence writer and a storyteller. And it began writing a letter to Steven Spielberg and writing something in Mr. Campo Piano's class and writing Dungeons and Dragons Adventures and a whole bunch of other things. But where else in the world was I beginning to tell stories? Not traditional on the page or even on the stage, but where in my life might I have been telling stories and taken another step on this path? And so I began thinking of the variety of contexts that I have occupied in my life. Where over the course of my life have I chosen to open my mouth and tell a story? So my son is a catcher on his baseball team now. Somehow he made himself into a catcher almost overnight. I don't know how he did it, but I am thrilled because I was a catcher in Little League. Charlie becomes a catcher and I watch his first game and I see that he's talking to the batter and he's talking to the umpire. I can't hear what he's saying, but I see that he's talking. I know what he's saying because I remember being a catcher too. I remember I was a catcher and the batter would come to the plate and I would say some of the most vile and foul things I could think of to throw that batter off. My whole goal actually was to make the umpire laugh. Every single time a batter came to the plate, my goal was to say things that were so awful or inane or inhumane that the umpire would find me funny. So when Charlie's game is done, he comes over to me and I say, what'd you say to the hitters? Did you swear at them? Did you make fun of them for striking out the previous at that? What terrible things were you saying to those batters? And Charlie says to me, dad, it's not the 1980s anymore. We're not terrible people like you were. We're better people than that. I'm really disappointed. I hate the way the world has changed, but as we're having this conversation, my wife says to me, so you were even telling stories on Little League Fields to make umpire laugh. You were basically doing comedy behind the plate. And I think, well, that's a context switch, isn't it? She's right. I was telling stories as a catcher on a baseball team to make an umpire laugh. My audience was one person wearing a mask and a chest protector and calling balls and strikes, but that one audience member meant the world to me. I wanted that guy to laugh based upon what I was saying to the kids standing next to me. That's context switching. I went to my scout camp with my kids, Yahoo Scout Reservation in Hopkington, Rhode Island, brought my kids to camp, took them around to show them the camp that I once occupied for most of my summers, my joyous boyhood adventure land, the place where I've asked my wife to spread my ashes in the very, very unlikely event I someday die. And we walk into the dining hall at Sandy Beach Dining Hall. It's this huge space with all of these tables. And then I see the stage and I remember, oh my God. When I was a kid, I was the one who used to call the tables into the kitchen to get their food. Table two, it's time to go into the kitchen. But I was also the kid who in between table two, it's time to go in the kitchen, cracked jokes about the people who were sitting in front of me and told stories about what happened in metalwork that day or the ridiculous fishing story that someone tried to tell me about their five pound bass that was probably a sunfish. I stood on that stage as a kid and I performed. Never occurred to me, but I was performing. A context switch, summer camp, me calling cafeteria tables into the kitchen and somehow also finding a way to find my audience and begin telling stories. I found another place, another context where I could create another bit of my origin story. About four months ago, I'm in Arizona. I'm in an Uber. I'm driving through Scottsdale, Arizona towards the hotel. And I'm making the Uber driver laugh. And as we're driving through Scottsdale and I'm listening to him laugh at the stories I'm telling and the jokes I'm telling, it occurs to me, I'm always desperately trying to make Uber drivers laugh. I'm always telling them stories. I asked that Uber driver in Scottsdale, do people talk to you as much as I'm talking to you? And he laughs again and he says, sometimes but not very often and they don't usually entertain me like this. And it occurs to me, I've spent my entire life making Uber drivers laugh because it's an audience. It's a captive audience of one. And I am continuing to be the storyteller and the sentence maker in the back of an Uber. It's not a stage, it's not a page. It's the backseat of a car. And I've got an audience member who can't even escape me if he wanted to. It's another context. It's another slice in my origin story. It's another example of me taking a tiny slice out of my life by switching contexts, by thinking about where am I a sentence maker that does not occupy the page or the stage. Your job is to do the same thing. Whatever you do, whether you're a carpenter or a plumber or a coder, you're launching a brand new business that's going to somehow paint houses using drones. Whatever it is, think about where else are you using the skills that you're using in your business? Where else in your life have you used those skills in a place that you wouldn't normally think? If you're a carpenter, try to get away from the wood. Get away from the hammers and the nails and the saws. If you're a carpenter, you're a person who builds stuff out of other stuff. Where else in your life have you been building stuff out of other stuff that is unrelated to wood and hammers and nails and saws? That's part of your origin story. If you are the person who is somehow creating a company that's going to paint houses with drones, you're a person who's going to be programming things to do other things for you. Where in your life, context switching, have you found a way to get things to do things for you? Where have you gotten people to do things for you? Because that's essentially what you're going to do. If you're a drone painting company, you are delegating machines to do the jobs that human beings were once doing. Where else in your life have you delegated? If you are a ritualistic delegator in your home, making and forcing your children to empty the dishwasher every single day because you see those small human beings as highly effective dishwasher emptiers, that's part of your origin story. That is you relentlessly delegating work to people who are not you. You're essentially doing the same thing in your business and that can be a really entertaining story, a really entertaining slice of your life. So, context switch, go through your life mentally. Think about all the things you've ever done. Think about all the places you've ever been. Think about all the roles that you have occupied. Find those roles and find the moments within those roles, within those differing places and differing experiences where the same skills that you applied to the work you do today were once also applied. When we can do that, our origin stories become really fascinating. They become diverse, they become entertaining and most important, they become different than most other people's origin stories. Most people are not looking to context switch when finding their origin story. If you can do that, you will differentiate yourself from most people and as storytellers, that's all we really wanted to. We want to be different than the other person who's speaking. Find ways to do it by context switching. All right, number three. The third strategy I want you to do to point, interview other human beings in your life. We are often blind to what we do. We don't see the things that we do and we don't recognize it as important even when we see it. And so the people in your life can often be enormously valuable when it comes time to finding your origin story. I have asked countless people in my life, countless times to talk to me about me. It doesn't sound like the healthiest thing to do. It doesn't make you sound like the best human being in the world, but the results are extraordinary. You find new slices from your life. So there's a day when I'm talking to a group of people, I've just published a book and they recognize me as an author or a storyteller and someone asks me, how did you get into storytelling? It seems like he got really good really quick. And I say, yeah, it's true. I went to New York City. I told my first story on the stage. I won my first Moth Story Slam and I was off and running. I've won more than half the competitions I've ever been in. I never took a class. It was just great fortune, I said, that I stumbled upon something that I was good at a little later in life. And from the back of the room, I heard a laugh. It was my wife. And I said, what? And she said, is that really what you think? Do you really think that you went to that Moth Story Slam and just found something you were naturally good at? And I said to her in front of a hundred people, well, what was it? And she said to me, Matt, you've been writing every single day of your life since you were 17 years old. Since that moment in Mr. Campos class, I have not missed a day of writing. She said, you've been writing stories and eventually novels forever. Do you really think that when you took that stage at that Moth Story Slam, that all of that storytelling and writing and publishing of novels didn't sort of help you out when you were standing in front of the microphone? And honestly, up until that point, I had never even considered it. But I thought, oh, she's probably right. And she goes, that's not all. And I was like, oh, God, what else? She said, Matt, you've been a wedding DJ for 25 years. Every Saturday, you stand in front of an audience of people who really don't want to listen to you and you have learned how to speak extemporaneously and not care what other people think. You don't think that helped you in New York City that night when you stood in front of a microphone that you had been standing in front of microphones for 25 years beforehand? And honestly, I thought to myself, ah, I think she might be right. That might have been helpful. And then she said, that's not it. And I was like, Jesus, what else? And she said, Matt, for the last 20 years, you've been an elementary school teacher. You stand in front of the worst audience on the planet, 10-year-old children. And she knows the way I teach is every single day I tell them stories. I tell them stories to keep them entertained because children who are entertained at school learn better. That is my secret sauce. She said, Matt, when you took the stage that night, those three things came together as one. And that is why you're winning. You've been practicing to become a storyteller for the last 25 years. You just didn't know it. And in that moment, I had another slice of my origin story. Three enormous things from my life that I never considered to include in my life. Three things that my wife gave me that night. I'll give you one more. About a year ago, I received an envelope in the mail from my aunt Diane, the aunt on my mother's side. She lives on Cape Cod. I don't see her very much at all. We occasionally exchange emails. But on this day, a package has arrived, a big yellow envelope. I open it. It has a letter in it. The letter says, Matt, when you were 12 years old, on Easter, you were at Meme and Pepe's house, her parents' house, my grandparents' house. She said, you were sitting at a coffee table, and you had paper and pencils, and you were drawing cartoons. She says, everyone else was at the table talking, and you were all by yourself drawing cartoons. And I wandered over, and I looked, and I saw what you were drawing. And I tried to point it out to your parents, and to my parents, and to your aunts and uncles, and no one seemed to think it was anything special. But she says, I thought it was special. So she says, when you got up from the table, I collected all of those papers, and I've held on to them for the last 40 years. And I just stumbled upon them, and I thought it would be important for you to have them. So I start going through the papers. They're comics. They're political cartoons. At the age of 12, I am sitting on Easter Sunday at a coffee table, and I am writing political cartoons about my criticism of Reaganomics, of trickle-down economics, of inflation, and unemployment. At 12 years old, somehow, and I don't remember doing it, I am understanding, commenting, and criticizing the politics of the president of the United States, Reaganomics. That is absolutely a slice of bread. It is absolutely a moment that I did not have. I did not remember. My aunt Diane sends me a package, essentially an interview. And as a result, I have this extraordinary story to tell. Interview the people in your life, everyone. Interview them more than once. Ask them about yourself often. Ask them to tell you things about yourself that you don't know, and maybe even things that you do know. They'll explain them through a lens that you perhaps can't imagine. And then you'll have more stories. The goal in all three strategies, expand the amount of time that you're looking through. Expand the context to which you're looking, and ask people to help you find stories that are meaningful. Those three things, the goal is to assemble an enormous pile of stories. Origin stories, moments in your life, steps on the path that have brought you to the moment you're in today. So you've got all of these stories to tell now. Each one essentially represents a step along the path to where you are today. Why do we have all these stories? What is the benefit of having all these stories? Well, there's a bunch of them. The first is, you always want to be considering your audience when you're telling a story. And so if you have a singular origin story, that means that the audience becomes irrelevant to you, and that is disastrous. That essentially means it doesn't matter whether I am speaking to 10-year-old children or blue-haired old ladies, they're all going to get the same story. It means that you're telling the same story to someone who has $5,000 to invest in your company, and someone who has $15 million to invest in your company. You can see that that would be disastrous. Storytellers want to have stories that match the audiences to whom they're speaking. It is the first question I ask whenever I'm getting ready to tell a story. Who's the audience? Because that will determine my content, and it should for you too. If you have one origin story, you tell them the same one every time, and you're failing most of the time. So having a multitude of stories is enormously powerful when it comes to needing to tell someone your origin story. Number two, you don't always have to tell the same story. If you're a business leader of any kind, or a creator of any kind, if you're appearing on podcasts, television interviews, radio interviews, you don't want to be the person who tells the same damn story every time. And there's a lot of people in the world who tell the same damn story every time. When I'm interviewed on a podcast and someone says, how did you get your career in storytelling, Matt? Sometimes I tell them about Spielberg. Sometimes I tell them about Mr. Campo. Sometimes I tell them about making umpires laugh. Sometimes I tell them about Shep and Dungeons and Dragons. They get different stories. So if you hear me in one place and you hear me in another place, you're often hearing different stories. That's enormously valuable for me, because it both makes me more entertaining. It shows a variety of sides of who I am, so that people can experience me as both a kid who loved movies, a kid who was lost in high school and a lot of trouble, a guy who has one of the most precious friends you could ever have. It begins to allow people to see me in a different light, in different contexts. It expands their view of me and makes me seem multi-dimensional, which I am. So having a multitude of stories means I have a multitude of stories, and I get to tell different stories every time. But here's the best reason why you want to have as many of these slices of bread as possible, because sometimes you're going to tell a longer version of your origin story. But when you have all of these laid out in front of you, you get to connect them in any way that you want. So one version of my origin story might be Spielberg to Mr. Compo to Shep. Another version of my origin story, though, might be Aunt Diane's political cartoons, up to my wife pointing out to me that being a wedding DJ was really important to me, becoming a storyteller, to maybe the first night I ever told a story at a Moth's Story Slam. Every single time I'm asked to tell an origin story, I get to collect all of these pieces of bread and reassemble the loaf of bread that is my life in a multitude of ways. It means that every single time I have to tell that story, I get to tell a different story. The components might be repeated every now and again, but the connection of the two or the three or the five is going to be different. And people are going to get different stories each time. And if I'm strategic and tactical, which I am and you should be too, I'm looking at my audience and saying to myself, which three steps along my path are the three steps that these people want to hear? Which three steps along my path are going to be the ones that influence them, impact them, make me unforgettable? Which three steps along my path are going to tell the story about my business in a way that's going to land perfectly with the customer, the investor, the person looking to work for my company? Which three are going to serve me best? You don't want one story. You want to have the Swiss Army Knife of origin stories. You want to have a multitude of ways to reassemble the moments in your life in a way that makes the most sense for both you and, more importantly, the people you're speaking to. So when we're looking to find our origin story, we stop doing that. And instead, we find our origin stories. We expand our timeframes. We expand our contexts. And we ask others to help us find the right stories. And then, once we have all of those pieces in front of us, we can assemble them. We can learn how to tell each one well so that they can operate singularly as individual stories and as interconnected pieces in your origin story. And then, if you're ever lucky enough to do a podcast like How I Built This with Guy Ross, an hour and a half interview, then maybe in those very rare instances, you gather most of your slices of bread. And with the help of someone like Guy Ross, an expert interviewer who's going to cut and edit your story to make you sound great, you get to bring in most of them. But almost all of the times, we're not telling a story that's an hour and a half. We're not serving up a full loaf of bread. What we're doing is we're gathering up a few pieces. We're collecting two or three moments that tell the story of how we became who we are, how our company became what it is today. Not all of them because nobody wants all of your story. They want interesting, entertaining, impactful, unforgettable moments along the path, but not all the moments. So go to work. Start by laying out all those stories. Expand your time horizon and think about every moment in your life through the lens of what you do today. Think about all of the contexts of your life, places, experiences, circumstances, through the lens of what you do today. Ask your friends to talk about you through the lens of who you are today. Collect those stories, figure out how to tell them, and begin assembling them so that you have all the origin stories you will ever need for every moment that someone asks you, what's your origin story?
